sentences.text: [TONE]
Sabina Heller.
H-E-L-L-E-R.
Ina Goszczewski.
G-O-S-Z-C-Z-E-W-S-K-I.
No.
Yes.
Inka, Inousha, Ineshka.
[LAUGHS]
August 1, 1941.
In Lodz.
Poland.
L-- it's L, actually-- O-D-Z.
Mhm.
Probably when the family that I lived with them-- a Polish family-- was telling me that when I grow up, they have a letter for me that they want to show me.
And I really didn't know what they were talking about.
But I do remember that that's probably the first incident that I remember.
I remember a little yellow--
Well, I was with them until the age of six, so it must have been what, maybe five.
I don't know how far back do kids remember, you know?
But maybe five, maybe five and a half.
You know.
Well, it's like, you know, very vague.
You know?
I remember that they had grown up kids.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
I remember a yard, and I remember the man of the family, that he was chopping wood.
I don't know why I remember that.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's all.
You know, it's just like a big mist.
I remember-- what else-- I remember also going to kindergarten, starting school.
I remember crying terribly, not wanting to go.
And I remember walking through a long field.
Walking over towards the-- towards the school.
And I remember there was a-- that-- that school would start with a prayer, and there was the priest that we would, you know, say the prayer before we started school.
That's-- that's what I-- Then-- then I--
Did I know that?
What?
No, I had no idea.
I just--
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
And I remember, they-- they were Catholic, and-- and they took me to church.
[SIGHS] I don't re-- I just don't remember much.
I don't know if there is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] why I can't remember that much.
Do you-- But as we progress with my life story, there were so many things that I went through that I guess, it's just so far away.
You know?
Or maybe it's some kind of a defense mechanism, that you know, that-- Do you remember a birthday?
Because I was-- hm?
A birthday?
Oh no, I don't remember any birthdays at all.

I imagine they treated me well, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] basically I don't-- I mean I have some fears, but I didn't have any terrible trauma where, you know, it was an impediment in my growth, or in my schooling, or in my relationship with other people, so.
But I don't have any details.
I wish I did, but I don't.
Yeah, that's-- I do remember going to school.
I don't remember much, just that lesson with the priest.
But otherwise, I don't remember much.
And I don't even know if I did the whole academic year there, or it was just a few months.
Because like, at about the age of six is when-- you know, when they told me that they're going to take me on a trip.
We're going to go on the train, and they're going to take me to the big city.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took me on the train.
And they never told me why or what, absolutely nothing.
Or maybe I don't remember.
But I don't remember them telling why are we going there.
And--
Yeah, it was a house, but I have absolutely no details.
No, no.
I don't.
I don't remember.
Yeah.
And they took me-- we arrived to a-- a Jewish orphanage in Lodz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't remember, at all, saying goodbye to them.
At all.
It's just-- I guess it was about-- it was about November.
Because I do have a picture from a Hanukkah party in the Jewish orphanage.
And I don't remember them saying goodbye to me.
They just disappeared from my life.
And then I-- my life started in the Jewish orphanage, where, you know, there were a lot of-- I don't remember if there were boys there, or there-- I don't remember boys.
I think they were girls.
And we-- I remember there was a big, big room with a lot of beds.
You know, and we sort of, you know, jumped around and played with the pillows and, you know, talked.
And--
Yeah.
I was happy there.
I don't remember who was in charge, but I think they treated me really nice and well.
And-- and I do remember there was a woman doctor there.
And she-- every time I-- wherever I was, there she popped somewhere near me, trying to make a conversation with me.
And I sort of resented it at first, because I just wanted to be an orphan like everybody else.
[LAUGHS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, that person was my mother.
And she's the one who-- she worked in that orphanage as a doctor.
And you know, there was a lull right after the war, where all the Polish-- all the Jewish kids that were hidden wherever, you know.
I'm certainly not the only case.
You know.
Hidden by Polish families, or hidden by churches, or wherever-- you know, whoever saved their life, they had to return them.
You know, if they had-- If their parents survived, or a relative survived.
So in other words, even though those people probably got attached to those-- to those kids, I'm sure that the people that raised me, that Polish family, maybe they didn't want to give me back.
I wouldn't be surprised.
After all, I spent with them quite a few years, and I came there just as a baby.
But there was a law that required them to return those Jewish kids.

And I imagine that that's how-- I-- I imagine that when my parents survived, there was a Jewish community center, where everybody that survived would come and write their name on the big board, and then people would come there and see who's alive, who's not, and-- and I guess this is how they inquired-- they-- they wanted me returned.
You know?
And so I was brought to the Jewish orphanage.
You know why they brought you to the Jewish orphanage?
Well, I think they brought me there because my mother was there, and she wanted--
My real mother, yes.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You know--
Well, she was very sweet, and she gave me a lot of attention.
And she talked to me, and then eventually she said that she's going to-- she's taking me home.
Yes.
Yes.
I don't remember exactly when it happened, but I do remember her telling me that.
And you know, with all the things that were happening in my life, I mean, I don't know.
It just-- things were just happening, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

As a matter of fact, you know, later on, in Israel, and I was talking to people, all kinds of little-- little things came up that-- I remember, somebody told me in Israel, what makes you think that these are really your parents?
Well, I don't know.
[LAUGHS] They told me they are.
But that's as much as-- I had no way of knowing.
That's what I was told, that they're my parents.
And-- and now, looking back at it, of course, these are the only parents I have, so they are my parents.
You know?
And it really makes no difference who gave you birth, you know, after all, it's who takes care of you that makes a difference.
And basically, I'm their product.
You've discussed it with your mother?
What?
Well, you know-- no.
I don't think I really wanted to know.
It was-- everything was so confusing.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came home, and of course, basically this is where my life starts.
This is where my-- this is-- from this moment on, I do remember things.
So.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a wonderful life.
You know, my parents showered me with love.
We are talking about 1948.
And I came back home, and they showered me with love.
And I remember my parents-- my mother putting me to bed and singing me a lullaby.
And-- you know, and talking, and playing, and friends are coming over.
And then when I was sick, I mean, I was the queen.
And people came and wrote me games and played with me.
And I didn't have, like, little friends that I played with, you know, but I remember getting a lot of attention and a lot of love.
And they put me in school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went there to first grade.
And I was a good student.
[LAUGHS] And I think somewhere I do have that certificate to even prove it.
[LAUGHS] And-- and about--
Well, where I was-- I suppose, I think where I-- the school that I went to was-- yeah, it was a Jewish school.
So I didn't have any problems there, definitely.
And I was very sheltered and protected.
I didn't experience anything bad in Poland.
And as a matter of fact, I'm glad you brought this subject of how were Jews treated there.
Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just feel this is the perfect time for me to bring up this issue, where so many Jews talk about anti-Semitism in Poland.
And I am the symbol of-- of the good-- of the good and-- and of the tremendous risk and help that I got from Polish people.
I survived thanks to them.
You know?
In Lodz, yeah.
So I did not-- I am nothing but grateful.
And as a matter of fact, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of the-- my son, my oldest son, Ron, he's really an expert on Holocaust.
And he has read so much about it.
And he says, mom, it is so unfair to what the Jews are saying about the Poles.
Because when we look at Yad Vashem, at the number of Poles that saved Jewish lives, there's more Poles that saved Jewish lives than any other--
Yeah.
I stayed in Poland until 1950.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then my parents, like many, many Jews at that time, decided to leave Poland and-- and make [?
Aliyah ?] go to Israel.
And so, you know, I remember clearly-- I remember clearly other Jews leaving Poland at that time.
Some of them were fortunate to come to this country, some went to Canada, and we went to Israel.
So we arrived in Israel, I believe it was January 1950.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And you know, so talk about changes, now I had to start all over again.
You know.
And I had very strong Polish R's.
You know?
And oh, I remember that it right away, give me away, that I was from Poland.
And that was the time when everybody wanted to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] be an Israeli.
Not to be from somewhere, but being a native Israeli.
And so I worked very hard on changing my Polish R to an Israeli R.
[LAUGHS] And--
I went to-- we came-- we stayed for about 10 days in tents.
It was called Ma'abarot.
Because at the time, that was the only way they could absorb all the newcomers that were coming.
And I remember raining, and we had a hole in the tent.
And my mother put a bucket to collect all the rain.
And we had some family there.
My family was the family of Yitzhak Rabin.
His father-- actually, his-- Yitzhak Rabin's mother is our relative.
Her and my grandpa were brother and sister.
And so Rosa Cohen, who's the mother of Yitzhak-- she was already dead.
She died at the age of 30 from lung cancer.
But her-- her husband, who is the widower, lived in Israel.
And he just, out the blue, came out with a pickup truck and took us to his apartment in Tel Aviv.
And you know, it was an amazing thing.
Because he just knew that he has relatives that survived the Holocaust.
He really didn't know us.
And I want to really give him credit for that.
It was an outstanding act of generosity on his part to take us in.
He has-- he had only a small apartment of two rooms, and he gave us one room, and he lived in the other one.
So we lived with him for a year.
[HEBREW] Rabin.
And a year later, you know, we moved to our own place.
And so I started-- when I came in 1950, I started elementary school.
I went into second grade.
I was the oldest kid in that grade.
I was there for about two months, and then my mother took me-- got me a private tutor during the summer, and I learned Hebrew.
My father-- of course, he was with us, and he got a job.
You know, we had some family that had a textile store, and he worked as a salesman there.
My mother immediately got a job as a doctor.
And so we started our new life, you know, in Israel.
And after that summer, I took a test, and I guess my Hebrew was good enough.
And I skipped third grade, went straight into fourth, and I was-- at that-- at the time, I was, like, on the same level of all the kids the same age.
From the minute that my feet stepped on the-- in Israel.
There was no way that you could not know you're Jewish.
It was a tremendous era of patriotism, of putting the-- what's good for the community before your own, you know, considerations.
100% Jewish.
No, no.
Because certainly, I was not the only newcomer immigrant.
That was-- everybody was from somewhere.
You know?
And so I was not unique, speaking Polish at home and speaking Hebrew at school.
And of course, I wanted to less and less of the Polish, and I started to talk more and more of the Hebrew.
No, I'm an only child.
Well, I was--
Well, I went to elementary school, then-- then I went to high school in Tel Aviv.
Keula ?] High School, which was a private school.
Because my grades were not that great.
[LAUGHS] I couldn't get into a-- in Israel, it was different.
Public school was the best, and private school was the second best.
So I couldn't get into public school, and I went to private school.
And I really became a good student in high school for some reason.
And when I graduated at the age of 18 from high school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to the Army.
I served for two years in [?
Sahan ?].
And then when I-- Did you ever encoun-- were part of a war there?
During my time, during my service, there was no war.
It was peacetime.
And I--
--ended-- I finished my military service as a Sergeant.
[LAUGHS] And-- but--

Then I-- you know, after two years in-- in the Army, which was, really, I have to say, it was a tremendous [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] experience that-- that really made me a responsible adult and also clarified to me what I really want.
Because when I finished high school-- high school in Israel is quite difficult.
There is these finals, and then-- and I didn't want to go on with school.
I thought, that's it.
I don't want to ever see another book.
Oh, yeah, definitely.
Well, he was-- when we came to Israel, he was already married then.
You know, he was a young man, but he was out of the house, and he was married.
And he lived in Tel Aviv, not far away, with his wife, Leah, who was an English teacher, and with their two kids.
He was a very quiet, shy man, who really didn't know how to make small talk.
And I always felt very awkward being with him.
[LAUGHS] Because here I was, a little kid, sitting with an-- you know, an adult, and he never tried to really-- you know, talk to me or make a conversation.
You know?
So I sort of had funny feelings about him at that time.
But I used to go and visit him.
I liked his little children.
And I remember his first trip out of Israel.
He didn't speak any English.
He was very, very apprehensive about it.
How he went to England to a military academy, and he was so worried how he's going to do there without the English.
And his wife tried to comfort him and-- and said, don't worry, I'll help you.
It's going to be OK.
And we know now that it was OK.
[LAUGHS]
Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let me just backtrack for just two sentences.
I just-- I did go to Hebrew University in Jerusalem.
And I graduated.
Got my BA in English Literature and Political Science and started working as a teacher in Israel.
An English teacher.
And then in 1970, I met my husband in Israel, who was visiting his family.
And you know, we got to know each other, and later on, I came back, and we got married.
And I came here, 1970.
With him.
Well, when I came here, I cried.
[LAUGHS] I was very-- it was very difficult for me to adjust here.
You know?
And I really-- it's-- it's coming from a small place, very warm and caring and-- and familiar, to a big, large, strange.
And so that was a tremendous, tremendous adjustment.
And what helped me was, of course, was I got a job right away.
About, I don't know.
A few weeks after I came.
I got a job in Stephen S.
Wise Temple.
It's a reform temple in Mulholland.
I started teaching Hebrew in Stephen Wise.
And then about a year later, I got pregnant.
And I had my-- my firstborn, Ron. 1971.
And I stopped working.
I was stay at home.
Being a homemaker for 10 years, and a mother.
I have two.
Then my younger one was born 1976.
His name is Mark.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when Mark was about three years old, I went back to work.
I went to teach part-time in Temple Judea.
I worked there for 10 years, teaching Hebrew.
I think that definitely contributed to all kinds of fears that I have.
[CHUCKLES] I'm always terrified of being alone.
So the abandonment is big fear.
And I'll do anything just not to be alone.
And [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I don't know how else-- I'm sure it affected me in many ways that I don't even know.
You know?
In the way-- yes.
I definitely wanted to be more for my kids, and I had my mother at home.
I certainly don't blame my mother, you know, because I know that these were years of struggle.
They just-- they needed both to work, and it was just-- she did what you could, you know?
And but she was a career woman, and it required a lot of time from her.
So I wanted to spend more time with my kids.
She was in different camps.
And my father was in camps, too.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And it is a great miracle that they both survived, because my father was handicapped.
His right hand was-- got caught in a wringer, you know?
And so he-- he survived.
It was a miracle.
And my mother survived thanks to the fact that she was a doctor, and they needed doctors, with all the diseases that they had there.
